rxn_class_summary: Integrated electrochemical-chemical oxidation of diarylmethanes, methylarenes, and aryl-substituted alkenes to carbonyl compounds (ketones, aldehydes, 1,2-diketones, and cyclized ketones) via anodically generated carbocations trapped by DMSO to form alkoxysulfonium ions, followed by Et3N-mediated Kornblum-type elimination.
electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation generates either a diarylcarbenium ion (cation pool, Method A, in CD2Cl2 at -78 °C) or a radical cation of an electron-rich arene/alkene (Method B, in DMSO/CD2Cl2) that is trapped in situ by DMSO to form an alkoxysulfonium ion; substrate is preferentially oxidized over DMSO because of its lower oxidation potential.

key_intermediates.name: 4-methoxybenzyl-type alkoxysulfonium ion (8, in situ trapped, d6-DMSO)
dialkoxysulfonium ion from stilbene (13) — bis-DMSO adduct of stilbene dication equivalent
cyclized alkoxysulfonium ion from alkene bearing pendant nucleophile (e.g., 24 from tosylamide-tethered styrene 23)
radical cation of styrene/diene precursor (proposed for cyclization substrates, e.g., 32•+)
distonic radical cation after intramolecular alkene cyclization

electron_flow: Anode removes one or two electrons from the substrate to give a benzylic/diarylcarbenium cation (or, for activated arenes/alkenes, a radical cation that is further oxidized after nucleophilic capture); DMSO traps the cationic carbon as an alkoxysulfonium ion, which Et3N then deprotonates to expel Me2S and yield the carbonyl product (Kornblum-type collapse).

probe_reagents.name: low-temperature 1H NMR characterization of cationic intermediates 2, 3, 8, 13, 24
probe_reagents.observation: Distinctive downfield H_a signals (e.g., 9.99 ppm for diarylcarbenium 2; 6.58 ppm for alkoxysulfonium 3; 5.17 ppm for 8; 5.93 ppm for 13; 6.13/3.68 ppm for cyclized 24) confirm accumulation of the proposed carbocation and alkoxysulfonium intermediates.

probe_reagents.name: rotating disk electrode (RDE) voltammetry of substrates
probe_reagents.observation: Eox values (e.g., 1.96 V for 1, 1.38 V for 7, 1.16 V for diene 32 vs 1.54 V for monoene 40) rationalize choice between Method A vs B and support rapid cation-radical cyclization for tethered dienes.

probe_reagents.name: comparison of Eox(32)=1.16 V vs Eox(40)=1.54 V (intramolecular vs single alkene)
probe_reagents.observation: Lower oxidation potential of the diene indicates that the initial radical cation undergoes rapid intramolecular cyclization onto the second alkene before further oxidation.

notes: Two operationally distinct modes: (A) 'cation pool' generation of stable diarylcarbenium at -78 °C in CD2Cl2 followed by DMSO addition, used when substrate Eox > Eox(DMSO)=1.76 V; (B) in situ DMSO trapping during electrolysis, used when substrate Eox < 1.76 V (e.g., 4-methoxytoluene, stilbenes, tethered alkenes), which also avoids accumulation of unstable cations (e.g., 4-methoxybenzyl cation). For cyclization substrates, the favored mechanism (Figure 4) is anodic one-electron oxidation to a radical cation, rapid intramolecular addition onto a second alkene/nucleophile to give a distonic radical cation, then a second anodic oxidation and DMSO trapping to give the cyclic alkoxysulfonium; Et3N then gives the cyclic ketone. The DMSO/Et3N step is mechanistically the Swern/Kornblum-type collapse of an alkoxysulfonium.
